summary: This early technical post addresses a common ASP.NET deployment problem where ViewState validation fails in web farm environments. The issue stems from each server auto-generating unique machine keys, causing ViewState created on one server to be rejected by another. Atwood explains two solutions: synchronizing machine keys across all servers, or disabling ViewState MAC validation entirely. The post ends with a sardonic quip about security, hinting at the trade-off involved in the easier solution.
key_points: ASP.NET ViewState is cryptographically tied to its originating server by default, despite documentation suggesting otherwise
Web farm deployments require either synchronized machine keys or disabled ViewState MAC validation
The easier solution (disabling enableViewStateMac) comes with security implications that shouldn't be ignored
Service Pack 1 improved error messages to actually explain the problem, a small but meaningful debugging improvement
money_quotes: ASP.NET ViewState is tied to the particular server it came from by default – even though the documentation says it isn't.
This is done to prevent users from somehow tampering with the ViewState. Any change to the ViewState data on the client will be detected.
Either way, works great. Who needs all that stupid security anyway?
key_insight: Web farm ViewState failures occur because ASP.NET's security-by-default approach conflicts with distributed deployment, forcing developers to choose between synchronized configuration or reduced security.
